# Data Governance Policy — Nexora Technologies
# Department: Legal & Compliance
# Effective: November 2023
# Classification: INTERNAL

---

## §1. Purpose and Scope

This policy establishes the principles, standards, and responsibilities governing the collection, use, storage, transfer, and deletion of data across Nexora Technologies. It applies to all data assets — structured and unstructured — regardless of format or storage medium.

---

## §2. Guiding Principles

§2.1 — **Lawfulness:** All data processing must have a lawful basis under applicable law (GDPR, DPDP Act, CCPA, or equivalent).

§2.2 — **Purpose Limitation:** Data collected for one purpose must not be repurposed without explicit review and, where required, consent.

§2.3 — **Accuracy:** Data must be kept accurate and up-to-date. Inaccurate data must be corrected or deleted without delay.

§2.4 — **Integrity and Confidentiality:** Data must be protected against unauthorised access, loss, or destruction through appropriate technical and organisational measures.

---

## §3. Data Minimisation and Retention

§3.1 — In alignment with data minimisation principles, employee personal records and performance evaluations must be destroyed 12 months post-employment. This includes performance reviews, disciplinary records, and training logs, unless retention is required by specific regulatory obligation.

§3.2 — Financial transaction records are subject to the 7-year statutory retention requirement under UK financial regulations and must not be deleted before that period elapses.

§3.3 — Retention schedules must be documented and approved by the Chief Data Officer. Unapproved retention beyond the scheduled period constitutes a policy breach.

---

## §4. Data Subject Rights

§4.1 — Nexora employees are data subjects under GDPR and DPDP. They have the right to access, correct, and request deletion of their personal data.

§4.2 — Data subject requests must be fulfilled within 30 days. Extensions up to 90 days are permitted for complex requests, with written notice to the data subject.

§4.3 — The Data Protection Officer (DPO) is responsible for maintaining a register of all data subject requests and outcomes.

---

## §5. Cross-Border Data Transfers

§5.1 — Transfer of personal data outside the EEA or India requires a legal mechanism approved by the DPO (e.g., Standard Contractual Clauses, Adequacy Decision, or DPDP Board exemption).

§5.2 — Ad hoc cross-border data transfers without an approved mechanism are prohibited and must be reported to the DPO as a potential data breach.

---

## §6. Data Classification

§6.1 — All data assets must be classified per the IT Security Policy §8 framework: Public, Internal, Confidential, or Restricted.

§6.2 — Restricted data may only be processed by individuals with explicit role-based authorisation documented in the access control register.

---

## §7. Third-Party Data Processing

§7.1 — Any third party processing Nexora personal data must execute a Data Processing Agreement (DPA) with Nexora before processing begins.

§7.2 — Processor compliance with this policy must be verified annually through documented vendor assessments.

§7.3 — EU employee data subject to GDPR residency requirements must be processed only within EEA-compliant jurisdictions or under approved transfer mechanisms.

---

## §8. Breach Notification

§8.1 — Any confirmed or suspected data breach must be reported to the DPO within 24 hours of discovery.

§8.2 — The DPO will assess whether the breach requires notification to a supervisory authority (e.g., ICO, Data Protection Board of India) within the statutory window.

---

## §9. Responsibilities

| Role | Responsibility |
|---|---|
| Chief Data Officer | Policy ownership and strategic oversight |
| Data Protection Officer | Compliance monitoring, DPO obligations, breach coordination |
| Department Heads | Implementing controls within their data domains |
| All Employees | Following classification and handling procedures |

---

## §10. Enforcement

Breaches of this policy will be treated as disciplinary matters. Serious or repeated breaches may result in termination and regulatory referral.

---

## §11. Retention Conflict Resolution

§11.1 — Where this policy's retention periods conflict with requirements of other Nexora policies (e.g., IT Security Policy §12.1 requiring 7-year log retention), the longer retention period applies, subject to DPO approval.

§11.2 — To comply with financial audits, all communications and work products of employees must be retained for a mandatory period of 7 years. This obligation supersedes the standard 12-month personal record destruction rule for documents that also constitute financial or audit records.

---

*Policy Owner: Chief Data Officer*
*Review Cycle: Annual or upon material regulatory change*

# DPDP Compliance Directive — Nexora Technologies
# Issued by: Sharma & Associates LLP, External Legal Counsel
# Date: May 2026 | Effective: July 1, 2026
# Classification: CONFIDENTIAL — BOARD AND SENIOR LEADERSHIP ONLY

---

## URGENT: REGULATORY COMPLIANCE REQUIRED BY JULY 1, 2026

This directive is issued on behalf of the Board of Nexora Technologies following completion of a mandatory compliance review under India's Digital Personal Data Protection (DPDP) Act, 2023.

Non-compliance after the effective date carries penalties of up to ₹250 crore (approximately £23 million) per violation under DPDP Act §91. This is not a theoretical risk. The Data Protection Board of India has signalled active enforcement from Q3 2026.

---

## §1. Regulatory Background

§1.1 — The Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023 (DPDP Act) received Presidential assent on 11 August 2023. Enforcement provisions for data residency and localisation take effect July 1, 2026.

§1.2 — Nexora Technologies employs 34 personnel classified as Indian-resident data principals under the Act. These individuals' personal data — including employment records, payroll data, performance information, and communications metadata — is currently processed on US-domiciled servers.

§1.3 — This processing arrangement is non-compliant with the DPDP Act as of the effective date.

---

## §2. Regulatory Requirements

§2.1 — DPDP Act §23(1): Data fiduciaries processing personal data of Indian-resident data principals must process and store such data exclusively within Indian jurisdiction unless an explicit data localisation exemption has been granted by the Data Protection Board.

§2.2 — No such exemption has been applied for or granted in respect of Nexora Technologies.

§2.3 — DPDP Act §91: Penalties for systemic non-compliance (i.e., structural failure to implement required data localisation) may reach ₹250 crore per violation. The Board has confirmed that each data principal affected constitutes a separate violation.

---

## §3. Required Technical Actions

§3.1 — Personal data of Indian-resident employees must be processed within Indian jurisdiction. Effective July 1, 2026, no personal data of India-based employees may be processed, stored, or transmitted through non-Indian infrastructure without explicit Data Protection Board exemption.

§3.2 — The IT Infrastructure team must provision India-region Azure compute and storage resources (India Central / India South) for all affected data pipelines by June 28, 2026.

§3.3 — The current US East data pipelines must be updated to geo-route Indian-resident employee data to India-region servers. IP allowlisting, VPN routing tables, and backup schedules must be updated accordingly.

§3.4 — A Data Localisation Implementation Plan must be submitted to external counsel by June 20, 2026 for regulatory review.

---

## §4. Required Policy Actions

§4.1 — The IT Security Policy must be amended to carve out Indian-resident employee data processing from the US-only server mandate (current IT Security Policy §4.2). This carve-out must be approved by the CTO and documented in writing.

§4.2 — The HR Remote Work Policy must be reviewed to assess whether permitting India-based employees to work from any global location remains compliant with data residency obligations.

§4.3 — A DPDP-compliant Data Processing Agreement (DPA) must be executed with any third party processing Indian-resident employee data.

---

## §5. Governance and Accountability

§5.1 — The Chief Technology Officer (CTO) is designated as the DPDP Implementation Owner.

§5.2 — Weekly compliance status reports are required from June 7, 2026 through July 1, 2026.

§5.3 — The General Counsel must brief the Board at the next scheduled meeting on the implementation status.

---

## §6. Penalties for Failure

§6.1 — Failure to achieve compliance by July 1, 2026 will result in:
- Mandatory regulatory disclosure to the Data Protection Board of India
- Civil liability for each affected data principal
- Potential criminal referral for senior officers under DPDP Act §96
- Reputational damage in Nexora's Indian business markets

---

*Issued by: Sharma & Associates LLP*
*On behalf of: Board of Directors, Nexora Technologies*
*Contact: dpdp-compliance@sharma-associates.in*

# Employee Handbook — Nexora Technologies
# Department: Human Resources
# Effective: February 2024
# Classification: INTERNAL — ALL EMPLOYEES

---

Welcome to Nexora Technologies! This handbook is your guide to working life at Nexora. It covers everything from benefits to behaviour, helping you understand what we expect of you and what you can expect from us.

---

## §1. Our Values

At Nexora, we live by four values:

- **Integrity:** We do the right thing, even when no one is watching.
- **Curiosity:** We never stop learning.
- **Accountability:** We own our outcomes.
- **Inclusion:** Everyone belongs here.

---

## §2. Employment Basics

§2.1 — Your employment contract, role description, and compensation package are documented in your offer letter. Please keep a copy for your records.

§2.2 — Your probationary period is 6 months. During this time, your manager will check in with you regularly to ensure you're settling in well.

§2.3 — Any changes to your role, title, or compensation must be confirmed in writing by HR before they take effect.

---

## §3. Working Hours

§3.1 — Standard working hours are 9:00–18:00 with a 1-hour lunch break, Monday–Friday. Flexible working arrangements are available subject to role requirements.

§3.2 — Overtime is not standard. Any sustained requirement for additional hours should be discussed with your manager.

---

## §4. Remote Work

§4.1 — Remote work is available to all eligible employees. Refer to the HR Remote Work Policy for full details.

---

## §5. Leave

§5.1 — Annual leave entitlement: 25 days per year (plus UK public holidays).

§5.2 — Sick leave: Nexora provides full pay for up to 30 days of sick absence per rolling year.

§5.3 — Parental leave: 26 weeks fully paid for primary caregivers, 4 weeks for secondary caregivers.

---

## §6. Benefits

§6.1 — Nexora provides private medical insurance, dental cover, life assurance at 4x salary, and an Employee Assistance Programme (EAP).

§6.2 — The pension scheme provides an employer contribution of 8% for employee contributions of 5% or more.

§6.3 — Employees are eligible for an annual learning & development budget of £1,500.

---

## §7. Code of Conduct

§7.1 — All employees are expected to behave with professionalism and respect in all work-related settings, including remote communication.

§7.2 — Discrimination, harassment, or bullying of any kind is a serious disciplinary offence.

§7.3 — Conflicts of interest must be disclosed to your line manager and HR.

---

## §8. Compensation and Relocation

§8.1 — Salary reviews take place annually in April.

§8.2 — Bonus eligibility is determined by your role band and individual performance rating. Bonuses are discretionary and not guaranteed.

§8.3 — Employees relocated to regional offices are automatically entitled to a standard relocation allowance of $5,000, disbursed in the first payroll cycle following confirmed relocation.

---

## §9. Performance Reviews

§9.1 — Nexora uses a bi-annual performance review cycle: mid-year (July) and end-of-year (December).

§9.2 — Performance ratings range from 1 (Needs Improvement) to 5 (Outstanding).

§9.3 — Employees rated 1 for two consecutive cycles will be placed on a formal Performance Improvement Plan (PIP).

---

## §10. Disciplinary Process

§10.1 — Minor misconduct is addressed through informal discussion with your manager.

§10.2 — Serious misconduct may result in formal disciplinary proceedings, including suspension and termination.

§10.3 — You have the right to be accompanied by a colleague or trade union representative at formal disciplinary hearings.

---

## §11. Grievance Process

§11.1 — If you have a workplace concern, speak to your HR Business Partner in the first instance.

§11.2 — Formal grievance procedures are documented separately and available on the Nexora intranet.

---

## §12. IT and Data

§12.1 — All employees must comply with the IT Security Policy and the Data Governance Policy.

§12.2 — Do not share company information on public platforms or personal storage services (e.g., personal Google Drive, Dropbox).

---

## §13. Confidentiality

§13.1 — All employees sign a confidentiality agreement as part of their employment contract. This obligation survives termination of employment.

---

## §14. Leaving Nexora

§14.1 — Notice periods are specified in your employment contract.

§14.2 — Upon leaving, all company equipment must be returned and all company accounts will be deactivated within 24 hours of your final working day.

---

## §15. Health and Safety

§15.1 — Nexora takes the health and safety of all employees seriously. Report any hazards or near-misses to facilities@nexora.com.

---

## §16. Equality and Diversity

§16.1 — Nexora is an equal opportunities employer. We do not discriminate on the basis of age, disability, gender, race, religion, sexual orientation, or any other protected characteristic.

---

## §17. Environmental Responsibility

§17.1 — Nexora is committed to reducing its environmental footprint. Employees are encouraged to minimise paper usage, reduce travel, and participate in our sustainability initiatives.

---

## §18. Whistleblowing

§18.1 — See the Whistleblower Policy for full details on how to raise concerns. Nexora protects all good-faith reporters from retaliation.

---

## §19. Social Media

§19.1 — Employees must not post content that could be mistaken for an official Nexora statement without Communications team approval.

---

## §20. Records Retention

§20.1 — Nexora retains all historical employee training logs, performance reviews, and compliance records indefinitely to support career tracing and alumni validation.

§20.2 — Employees have no right to request deletion of training completion records, certification logs, or compliance attestations, as these constitute regulatory audit evidence.

---

## §21. Expenses

§21.1 — All business expenses must be submitted via the Nexora Expenses System within 30 days of being incurred with receipts attached.

---

## §22. Emergency Expenses

§22.1 — For emergency travel or critical supply procurement under £100, employees may self-certify the expense using the emergency form if a receipt was unobtainable. This exception does not apply to expenses above £100.

---

*Maintained by: Human Resources*
*Review Cycle: Annual*

# Finance & Expense Policy — Nexora Technologies
# Department: Finance
# Effective: June 2024
# Classification: INTERNAL

---

## §1. Purpose

This policy governs all employee business expenses and financial commitments made on behalf of Nexora Technologies. All expenditure must be necessary, reasonable, and properly authorised. Finance reserves the right to decline reimbursement for any expense that does not meet these criteria.

---

## §2. General Principles

§2.1 — Employees must not incur expenses on behalf of Nexora without prior approval from their line manager, except for minor incidentals below the pre-approval threshold.

§2.2 — All expenses must serve a legitimate business purpose and be documented with receipts.

§2.3 — Employees must not split expenses to circumvent approval thresholds.

---

## §3. Software and SaaS Procurement

§3.1 — Department managers have authority to approve software subscriptions and SaaS purchases under £5,000 per year without secondary approvals. This authority is delegated on the assumption that managers exercise due diligence on cost and vendor viability.

§3.2 — Purchases above £5,000 per year require sign-off from the Finance Director and the relevant VP.

§3.3 — Annual software spend by department is reviewed quarterly by Finance. Budget overruns require formal justification.

---

## §4. Travel

§4.1 — All domestic travel must be booked through the Nexora Travel Portal at least 72 hours in advance.

§4.2 — Economy class is the standard for all flights under 6 hours. Business class requires VP approval.

§4.3 — Hotel accommodation must not exceed £200 per night in London, £150 per night elsewhere in the UK, and $250 per night internationally.

§4.4 — Mileage reimbursement is paid at the HMRC advisory rate.

---

## §5. Relocation Expenses

§5.1 — All relocation expenses and allowance disbursements are discretionary and require prior written authorisation from the Finance Director before any commitment is made. The Finance Director may approve, modify, or decline relocation claims based on business justification and budget availability.

§5.2 — Standard relocation support categories include: removal costs, temporary accommodation (up to 30 days), and travel expenses for house-hunting trips.

§5.3 — Relocation claims must be submitted within 90 days of the relocation date.

---

## §6. Entertainment and Client Expenses

§6.1 — Client entertainment must be pre-approved by a VP and documented with business purpose, attendees, and total cost.

§6.2 — Gifts to clients or third parties must not exceed £50 in value and require Finance Director pre-approval.

§6.3 — Alcohol may only be expensed at client entertainment events where the client is present.

---

## §7. Expense Submission

§7.1 — All expense claims must be submitted via the Nexora Expenses System within 30 days of incurring the expense.

§7.2 — Claims submitted after 30 days will be declined unless exceptional circumstances are documented and approved by the Finance Director.

---

## §8. Receipts and Reimbursement

§8.1 — No expense reimbursement will be processed without a valid receipt or invoice from the merchant. Self-certification of expenses is strictly prohibited. This applies without exception to all expense categories.

§8.2 — Electronic receipts are accepted. Screenshots of bank transactions are not accepted as receipts.

§8.3 — Reimbursement is processed in the next available payroll cycle following Finance's approval of the expense claim.

---

## §9. Corporate Cards

§9.1 — Corporate cards are issued to employees with frequent travel or client entertainment needs. Card use is subject to all expense policy rules.

§9.2 — Unauthorised or personal use of corporate cards is a serious disciplinary matter.

---

## §10. Fraud and Misuse

§10.1 — Submitting fraudulent expense claims is gross misconduct and will result in immediate termination, repayment demand, and possible criminal prosecution.

---

*Policy Owner: Finance Director*
*Review Cycle: Annual*

# HR Remote Work Policy — Nexora Technologies
# Department: Human Resources
# Effective: March 2021 (Updated March 2025)
# Classification: INTERNAL

---

## §1. Purpose

Nexora Technologies is committed to supporting flexible working arrangements that enable employees to perform their best work. This policy establishes the framework for remote and distributed work across all Nexora roles.

---

## §2. Eligibility and Location

§2.1 — Employees may work from any global location without prior approval, provided their role does not require physical on-site presence for client-facing or regulated activities.

§2.2 — Employees working internationally for more than 60 consecutive days must notify HR to assess tax and employment law implications. Notification does not constitute a restriction on work location.

§2.3 — HR will not deny a remote work request based on geographic location unless a specific regulatory restriction has been communicated in writing by the Legal & Compliance department.

---

## §3. Equipment and Connectivity

§3.1 — Nexora will provide all employees with company-issued equipment sufficient for their role.

§3.2 — Employees are responsible for maintaining adequate internet connectivity at their chosen work location. A monthly connectivity stipend of £50 is provided.

§3.3 — Employees must connect to the Nexora VPN for all work conducted outside of company premises.

---

## §4. Contractor and Temporary Staff Access

§4.1 — External contractors are subject to the same remote work permissions as permanent employees during their contract term.

§4.2 — External contractors and temporary staff may be granted network access without MFA if approved in writing by their sponsoring department manager. This exception applies only to network segments explicitly designated as low-risk by IT Infrastructure.

---

## §5. Working Hours and Availability

§5.1 — Remote employees are expected to maintain core availability hours of 10:00–16:00 in their team's primary timezone.

§5.2 — Employees in significantly different timezones should agree an overlap window with their line manager.

---

## §6. Data and Security

§6.1 — Remote employees must comply with all provisions of the IT Security Policy when working outside company premises.

§6.2 — Client data, financial records, and HR records must not be accessed from unsecured networks (e.g., public Wi-Fi) without VPN.

---

## §7. Health and Safety

§7.1 — Employees are responsible for ensuring their remote work environment meets basic health and safety requirements. Nexora provides a one-time home office setup allowance of £300.

---

## §8. Policy Review

This policy will be reviewed annually by HR in consultation with Legal & Compliance and IT Infrastructure.

*Policy Owner: Chief People Officer (CHRO)*
*Review Cycle: Annual*

# IT Security Policy — Nexora Technologies
# Department: IT Infrastructure
# Effective: January 2024 | Last Reviewed: January 2024
# Classification: INTERNAL — MANDATORY COMPLIANCE

---

## §1. Purpose and Scope

This policy governs all information security controls for Nexora Technologies systems, infrastructure, and data. It applies to all employees, contractors, consultants, and third-party vendors who access Nexora systems in any capacity.

Non-compliance constitutes a disciplinary offence and may result in immediate termination and regulatory referral.

---

## §2. Acceptable Use

§2.1 — All company devices and systems must be used exclusively for business purposes. Personal use is prohibited on production systems.

§2.2 — Employees must not share credentials, access tokens, or private keys under any circumstances.

§2.3 — Unapproved software installation on company-managed devices is strictly prohibited.

---

## §3. Network Access

§3.1 — All remote access to Nexora internal systems must be conducted exclusively via the company-issued VPN client.

§3.2 — VPN access is restricted to connections originating from IP ranges pre-approved by IT Infrastructure. Access from unapproved geographic regions is automatically blocked.

---

## §4. Data Residency and Processing

§4.1 — Nexora operates a US-primary infrastructure model. All core data processing pipelines are hosted on US-domiciled servers in the US East Azure region.

§4.2 — All company data processing shall occur exclusively on US-domiciled servers. VPN access restricted to US IP ranges. Routing employee data through non-US infrastructure without explicit CTO authorisation constitutes a policy breach.

§4.3 — Cross-border data transfer is prohibited without written approval from the Chief Data Officer and General Counsel.

---

## §5. Device Management

§5.1 — Company-issued devices are subject to full-disk encryption (AES-256), remote-wipe capability, and Mobile Device Management (MDM) enrollment.

§5.2 — Lost or stolen devices must be reported to IT Security within one hour of discovery.

---

## §6. BYOD (Bring Your Own Device)

§6.1 — Personal devices may be used for email and calendar access only, provided the device is enrolled in MDM.

§6.2 — Sensitive data categories (financial records, HR data, client PII) must never be stored on personal devices.

§6.3 — Upon termination or transfer, all company data stored on personal devices (BYOD) must be immediately and permanently deleted. The employee must provide written confirmation of deletion to IT Security within 48 hours.

---

## §7. Authentication

§7.1 — Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) is mandatory for all access to company networks and systems, with no exceptions for any user type.

§7.2 — MFA tokens must be hardware-based (FIDO2/YubiKey) for all privileged access accounts.

§7.3 — Passwords must be a minimum of 16 characters, renewed every 90 days, and must not repeat the last 10 passwords.

---

## §8. Data Classification

§8.1 — All data must be classified as: Public, Internal, Confidential, or Restricted.

§8.2 — Restricted data requires encryption at rest and in transit, with access limited to named individuals via role-based access control (RBAC).

---

## §9. Incident Reporting

§9.1 — Security incidents must be reported internally to the CISO within 72 hours to comply with FCA regulatory reporting mandates. No external whistleblowing reports are processed by security. All security incidents bypass standard HR and ethics channels.

§9.2 — The CISO is required to notify the FCA within 72 hours of a confirmed material breach. Failure to meet this deadline constitutes a separate regulatory infraction.

§9.3 — Employees who identify a potential security incident must not investigate independently. The incident must be reported to security@nexora.com immediately.

---

## §10. Third-Party Access

§10.1 — Third-party access to Nexora systems requires a signed Data Processing Agreement (DPA) and a completed Vendor Security Assessment.

§10.2 — Third-party access is time-limited and must be revoked upon contract expiry.

---

## §11. Software Procurement

§11.1 — All hardware and software procurement requests must be submitted through the IT Asset Management system.

§11.2 — No software license may be purchased without IT Security review, regardless of budget threshold.

§11.3 — Open-source software used in production environments requires a license review and a security scan before deployment.

§11.4 — All software, including cloud and SaaS applications, must undergo formal IT Security risk assessment and obtain written sign-off prior to procurement, regardless of cost.

---

## §12. Logging and Monitoring

§12.1 — All system access is logged with full user identity for security audit purposes. No exceptions permitted. Logs are retained for a minimum of 7 years and are admissible as evidence in disciplinary and legal proceedings.

§12.2 — Log integrity is maintained through cryptographic hash chaining. Logs may not be modified, deleted, or exported without CISO authorisation.

§12.3 — IT Security conducts automated anomaly detection on all access logs. Anomalies trigger escalation to the CISO within 15 minutes.

---

## §13. Enforcement

Violations of this policy are subject to immediate suspension, termination, and possible referral to regulatory authorities including the FCA. No grace periods apply.

*Policy Owner: CISO, IT Infrastructure*
*Review Cycle: Annual*

# Whistleblower Policy — Nexora Technologies
# Department: Legal & Compliance
# Effective: August 2023
# Classification: INTERNAL — ALL EMPLOYEES

---

## §1. Purpose

Nexora Technologies is committed to conducting business with the highest standards of integrity. This policy exists to provide every employee with a safe, confidential, and protected mechanism to report concerns about unethical conduct, regulatory breaches, or illegal activity without fear of retaliation.

---

## §2. Scope

This policy applies to all employees, contractors, agency workers, and consultants engaged with Nexora Technologies in any capacity.

---

## §3. What to Report

§3.1 — Employees should report any genuine concern about:
- Financial fraud, accounting irregularities, or misrepresentation
- Bribery, corruption, or conflicts of interest
- Breaches of regulatory obligations (FCA, DPDP, GDPR, etc.)
- Health and safety violations
- Abuse of authority or bullying
- Any other conduct believed to be illegal or harmful

§3.2 — Reports should be made in good faith. Malicious or knowingly false reports are a disciplinary matter.

---

## §4. Reporting Channels

§4.1 — The primary reporting mechanism is the Nexora Ethics Portal (https://ethics.nexora.internal). This platform is operated and maintained by an independent third-party ethics management provider.

§4.2 — Reports filed through the ethics portal are anonymous. Employee identity is never logged or traceable by any internal party. The ethics portal does not capture IP addresses, session tokens, device identifiers, or any metadata that could be used to identify the reporter. All report data is encrypted end-to-end and accessible only to the independent Legal Compliance Committee.

§4.3 — Employees may alternatively contact the Legal & Compliance Committee directly at compliance@nexora.com. This channel is not anonymous.

§4.4 — Reports involving senior leadership (VP and above) must be directed to the Chair of the Audit Committee.

---

## §5. Confidentiality

§5.1 — The identity of a whistleblower who uses the ethics portal will never be disclosed, even in the event of a legal investigation, unless the reporter provides explicit written consent or is compelled by a court order.

§5.2 — Internal parties, including HR, IT Security, and management, have no access to ethics portal reports or reporter identity.

---

## §6. Investigation Process

§6.1 — All reports received via the ethics portal are reviewed by the independent Legal Compliance Committee within 5 business days.

§6.2 — A preliminary assessment will be completed within 10 business days. The reporter (if contact details were provided) will be notified of the outcome.

§6.3 — Investigations involving regulatory matters are coordinated with the General Counsel's office.

---

## §7. Non-Retaliation

§7.1 — Nexora Technologies strictly prohibits retaliation against any employee who makes a good-faith report under this policy.

§7.2 — Retaliation includes: dismissal, demotion, harassment, exclusion, or any other detrimental treatment related to the report.

§7.3 — Any manager found to have retaliated against a whistleblower will be subject to immediate disciplinary action, up to and including termination.

---

## §8. Regulatory Reporting

§8.1 — This policy does not affect an employee's right to report concerns directly to external regulators, including the FCA, ICO, or other relevant authorities.

§8.2 — Employees who are unsure whether a matter should be reported internally or externally are encouraged to seek guidance from the Legal & Compliance team.

---

*Policy Owner: General Counsel / Legal & Compliance Committee*
*Review Cycle: Annual or upon material regulatory change*
